summary: DHH responds to Charles Miller's exploration of Java's getter/setter verbosity problem. Miller proposed using a unified method that accepts a hash of attributes to assign them on an object instance, but dismissed his own idea. DHH argues Miller had the right approach but failed to recognize its potential, teasing a longer discussion on the topic.
key_points: Java's getter/setter pattern creates unnecessary verbosity that obscures intent
A hash-based mass-assignment approach is a viable shortcut for object attribute setting
Good ideas are sometimes discarded prematurely by their own creators
money_quotes: That's a shame because he had the right idea.
Unfortunately, he fails to see its uses and discards the idea with a clever Simpsons reference.
key_insight: The hash-based mass-assignment pattern that Java developers dismissed as impractical was exactly the kind of shortcut worth embracing — foreshadowing a core Rails convention.
